name: Sustainable Cities and Urban Ecologies

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The certificate offers a broad, current interdisciplinary menu around urban systems: planning theory (ARCH 636), land-use planning (ARCH 655), GIS and visualization (ARCH 677, ARCH 647), sustainable urbanism (ARCH 684), smart-city/ICT context (ARCH 688 The Augmented City), plus digital (DD 634, DD 640), UX (IS 661, IS 664), and finance (FIN 600, FIN 611). Coherence is diluted by two issues: the digital-product and UX electives pull away from the stated urban theme, and the ecological half of the title (green infrastructure, urban ecology) is barely represented, with the one plausibly environmental course (CE 655) actually missing from the catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Tighten the menu into 2-3 labeled tracks (planning/policy, urban data/tech, finance) and add genuine urban-ecology content so the 'ecologies' in the title is substantiated.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: deepestPrereqChain is 0, which suits a flat elective menu, but missingCourseCount is 1: CE 655 is a dangling reference absent from the catalog, and several courses carry only vague prose prerequisites (FIN 611's programming requirement; the DD courses' 'basic background in digital media'). A few descriptions also read 'Restriction: completion of core sequence' (ARCH 647, ARCH 651), a copy-paste artifact from the parent degree that makes no sense in a standalone certificate.
dimensions.suggestion: Resolve or replace the dangling CE 655 reference and strip the inapplicable 'completion of core sequence' restrictions carried over from the degree program.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: No program-level learning outcomes appear in the data, so the certificate does not state what a completer should be able to do across its wide-ranging menu.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish 3-5 learning outcomes tied to the sustainable-cities theme (e.g., analyze urban systems with GIS, evaluate sustainable land-use policy).

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: statedTotalCredits is null with no requirement groups, yet the menu lists 14 referenced courses; with no stated total and no required-versus-elective or choose-N guidance, a student cannot tell how many or which courses complete the credential, a larger transparency gap than for the smaller sibling certificates.
dimensions.suggestion: State the total credits and an explicit choose-N rule (e.g., complete 4 of the listed courses for 12 credits, one from each track).

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: Individual course descriptions are present and mostly informative, but the entry is an undifferentiated 14-course list with no plan of study, no credit total, no grouping, one dangling course (CE 655), and leftover degree-only restrictions in some descriptions, so it reads as an unfiltered menu rather than a curated credential.
dimensions.suggestion: Group the courses into labeled tracks with a short credential header (total credits, selection rule, audience) and remove artifacts.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The offering is current where it engages technology and sustainability: smart-city ICTs (ARCH 688), GIS (ARCH 677), sustainable urbanism (ARCH 684), fintech (FIN 611), and UX methods (IS 661, IS 664) reflect contemporary urban-systems practice. Currency is undercut by the thin ecological/climate-resilience content, which is central to how the field now frames sustainable cities.
dimensions.suggestion: Add climate-adaptation and green-infrastructure electives so the certificate reflects the field's current environmental-resilience emphasis, not only planning and digital tools.

descriptionQuality.justification: Most of the 14 descriptions are present and specific (e.g., ARCH 688's history of ICTs disrupting urban space; ARCH 655's land-use and population-study methods), though a few carry copy-paste 'Restriction: completion of core sequence' fragments from the parent degree that reduce their fit to a certificate context.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Clarity is mixed: one dangling reference (CE 655), several informal prose prerequisites (FIN 611's programming requirement, the DD courses' 'basic background in digital media'), and degree-only 'completion of core sequence' restrictions on ARCH 647/651 that do not apply to certificate students.

missingTopics.rationale: Despite 'Urban Ecologies' in the title, no course addresses ecosystem services, stormwater/green infrastructure, urban heat, or biodiversity; the one environmental course (CE 655) is missing from the catalog.
Flooding, heat, and sea-level-rise adaptation, central to sustainable-cities practice and acute for New Jersey, are absent from the current planning-and-tech menu.
Contemporary urban planning foregrounds equity and distributional impacts, which none of the listed policy or planning courses explicitly covers.
Sustainable mobility (transit, active transport, EV/charging networks) is a core lever for urban decarbonization but is not represented.
ARCH 677 provides GIS, but remote sensing, sensor networks, and urban data science that now drive city analysis are not offered.

emergingTrends.rationale: The field increasingly pairs ecological and engineered systems for stormwater and heat; this is exactly the missing 'ecologies' content the title promises.
ARCH 677 (GIS) and ARCH 688 (Augmented City) position the certificate to grow toward city-scale digital twins and scenario modeling.
Linking ARCH 684 sustainable urbanism to district-scale decarbonization and resilience reflects where sustainable-cities practice is heading.

emergingTrends.trend: Smart cities, IoT, and civic sensing
emergingTrends.rationale: ARCH 688 and DD 634 (physical computing) provide a base to modernize toward IoT-driven urban sensing and data governance.
IS 664 Customer Discovery and UX methods could be reframed toward participatory planning and community co-design, a growing practice area.

topSuggestions: Add genuine urban-ecology content (green/blue infrastructure, ecosystem services, climate adaptation) so the 'Urban Ecologies' half of the title is substantiated.
Resolve the dangling CE 655 reference and remove the degree-only 'completion of core sequence' restrictions carried into certificate course descriptions.
State the total credits and an explicit choose-N selection rule for the 14-course menu, ideally organized into labeled tracks.
Publish 3-5 program learning outcomes tied to the sustainable-cities theme.
Tighten the menu so digital-product/UX electives are framed as an urban-tech track rather than diluting the urban-systems focus.
